article_title: Northwestern Engineers Print Artificial Neurons That Talk to Living Brain Cells

content_preview.title: Northwestern Engineers Print Artificial Neurons That Talk to Living Brain Cells
content_preview.summary: Researchers at Northwestern University have used aerosol jet printing to create flexible artificial neurons that generate biologically realistic signals and directly activate living neural tissue.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Engineers at Northwestern University have printed flexible artificial neurons capable of generating electrical signals that directly activate living brain cells — a capability that had eluded researchers for decades. The devices, described in a study published April 15 in [Nature Nanotechnology](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41565-026-02149-6), are made from printable electronic inks and produce spiking patterns that fall within the temporal window required for biological interac...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-written 665-word News piece on Northwestern's printed artificial neurons. Good technical explanation of the MoS₂-graphene memristive approach, the partial polymer decomposition innovation, and the temporal window challenge. The 'What It Could Enable' section is appropriately forward-looking without overstating results. Strong 'What Remains to Be Done' section that honestly scopes the ex vivo limitations.
editor_notes.source_verification: 3 sources read. (1) Northwestern Now press release — confirms aerosol jet printing, MoS₂/graphene ink, flexible polymer substrates, mouse cerebellum tissue test, Hersam's Walter P. Murphy Professor title, Vinod K. Sangwan (research associate professor at McCormick), Indira M. Raman (Weinberg), '5 orders of magnitude more energy efficient' claim. Northwestern Now provides a different Hersam direct quote ('You can see the living neurons respond to our artificial neuron...we've demonstrated signals that are not only the right timescale but also the right spike shape'); the article's attributed quote ('We are within a temporal range that was not previously demonstrated for artificial neurons') is attributed to Interesting Engineering and confirmed there. (2) Nature Nanotechnology — paywalled primary source; not read directly, but cited with correct DOI (10.1038/s41565-026-02149-6) and paper title confirmed via press release. (3) Interesting Engineering — confirms the temporal range quote, organic (too slow) / metal oxide (too fast) comparison, MoS₂/graphene approach, mouse cerebellum test.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All verifiable claims confirmed. Minor note: the Hersam quote in the article is drawn from Interesting Engineering's reporting and may be a paraphrase rather than a verbatim quote from the primary source; Northwestern Now provides a different direct quote from Hersam. This is a common secondary-reporting situation, not a factual error. The 'memristor' characterization is accurate and supported by the source; Northwestern Now describes the memristive behavior (conductive filament formation) without using the term itself.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and scientifically accurate. Applications are presented as potential rather than achieved. Limitations are clearly stated.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald coverage of this research. Original and timely for an April 15 publication.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Approve. Well-sourced, accurate science news article. All major claims verified. Minor quote attribution note is non-blocking.
